item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, cursed immortality? That's a new one. He deadpans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Necro'd to say:

I don want to die.

That hit me like a brick, despite its simplicity. Have this upvote (and these shudders).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this have a law of diminishing returns thing going on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something about the phrasing of the discovery of the guy in the Description strikes me as a bit off, though I can't pick out why. >.>

Generally I like this, though. +1'ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Things are clearer now and the journal entries add a lot to the article. Reversed my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The revision is a clear improvement. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers! I did worry about that, which is why I added the 'errors author's own' bit. If anybody else mentions it I might add (sic) to each one to make it clearer, but hopefully it'll sit well as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Updated version upvoted. Well done.

P. S. the fragment at the end is memetic. I was compelled to press "Edit" and fix all the typos, until I saw that all the mistakes were there for a reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback, I think I'm going to drop or at least rewrite the line about the D-class. There is definitely a school of thought at the moment that pointless D-class attrition should be discouraged, and that sentence was trying to stay in line with that, but it does need rewording.

As for the mechanism reference, that has been almost universally unpopular, and that's going in the next draft, I intend doing some extensive rewrites to this in the near future, putting in a lot more anecdotal evidence from diaries and witness interviews, I hope that will make the intention (which you've nailed on the head with the existention terror line) more clear.

My intention was that the top span away from him when he tried to restart it, probably due to him fumbling it because of the injuries on his hand. It slid under the sofa, hit the back wall and stopped, and he died whilst scrabbling on the floor desperately trying to grab it so he could start it again. Getting that into the story might be trickier (he wouldn't have written a diary entry for that, I imagine!) but I think that police interviews by the neighbours might be a way of getting that in there, or at least enough hints for the reader to piece it together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The bit about "unavoidable impact to the D class population" just doesn't sit well with me.
For one, the top doesn't have any inherently bad effects, decidedly the opposite in fact. Yes, I can see how tests along the lines of "subject spins top, shoot subject in head, did the top protect the subject?" would be a large part of the testing. But that would only use up a handful of Ds before the facts were established, and the Foundation really doesn't care about conserving D-class numbers until the death-toll is measured in cattle-cars-full.

The bit about it being part of the Antikythera Mechanism sticks out like a sore thumb. It doesn't add anything and it doesn't relate to the effects. It doesn't take anything away either, but it just feels randomly tacked on.

Was the top still spinning when the cops found the body? It sounded that way to me, but a dead body would have to sit for more than 14 hours before smell could alert the neighbors. I hear IRL stories of old people being dead in their apartments for weeks before the aroma got bad enough to justify breaking in.

I strongly support a diary, it would not only clarify some of the details but could really play up what I feel is the true horror of this thing. That being the existential terror of looming death, held off in only the tiniest increments by the most (literally) unstable of methods.

+1 for a good idea competently done, but I still look forward to improvements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a more basic version that shows how it works a lot better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahhhh. I've never seen one of those and wondered what the crap the doohickey on the side was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For once I'm organised, as I already did that. Forgot other people didn't grow up near a Victorian museum like I did. :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Well, I suppose i'll try and work together a diary entry and perhaps an police interview of a neighbour into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That actually does make a lot more sense, and is a good story, and you're mistaken if you think it leaves nothing to the imagination…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got the string-spin from the start, but I also assumed the guy was trying to stop the top - Maybe it staarted spinning non-stop and he just wanted to die.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really wanted at least some of it left up to the reader's imagination, but I think I'm going to have to go with writing the guy's diary to save this SCP. Something like this:

He gets the spinning top when he's young. Idle spins it a lot, maybe uses it as a desk toy.

He gets something like osteoarthritis or a similar affliction, feels better when he's spinning it without realising, Pavlov's dog kind of thing.

He starts to grasp that there's some connection between him feeling better than many of his companions and the top. Finds out from crazy gypsy lady a folk tale about it.

Starts using it more often, uses it all the time.

Now this bit is tricky as he wouldn't be able to diarize it:

Realises that he's reached the end of his natural life and that he's dying having used up about 70 non-spinning years. He keeps the top spinning constantly to hold back death, but it gets harder and harder to spin it and it spins for less time as he gets closer to his natural end. Then he spins it too hard, causing a huge tear in the already blistered and torn palm. It spins under the sofa and stops against the back wall. In a panic he jumps down onto the floor and starts desperately scrabbling for it, dislocating his frail shoulder (he's 70 after all). His frantic actions may even speed up his descent into death (Oh, the irony!) and he passes away on the floor, the top just out of reach.

Now I've removed any possibility of the reader having to imagine anything at all, I hope it makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of a top that grants immortality in useless chunks of time leading to an obsessive use of it, and I like that the compulsion is likely natural rather than magical, but do keep working on the execution. I've upvoted on the premise, and I hope to see this get better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That doesn't make any goddamn sense. If he spun the top all his life then why did he die right after dropping the goddamn thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was ahead of its time in quality and precision, no doubt; the main reason that it stood alone was because the vast majority of the populace didn't even consider something like mechanical precision. If they were to go into a massive city temple for the first time, make an offering into a box, and suddenly fountains sprang to life and horns blared and two giant statues of the gods *came to life and pulled the doors open to reveal Zeus in all his glory*, people in ancient Rome would likely shit themselves in awe and terror, rather than ask (as we would today) where all the gears and ropes and such were hidden, which is totally how they did that sort of thing. So it's more a case of (to use TVTropes style phrasing) no blueprint, no prototype, no backup.

That said…. To the original topic, I do still like the CONCEPT of the thing you wrote up, just this bit here irked me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is something that must be mentioned in the report, as I'm fairly everyone else never realized it was a string-spun top, just something you'd fling with your fingers.

item.title: What in the...
item.content:encoded:
I got an Inception vibe from this. An Inception vibe. Why? From the little top spinny thing at the end of the movie, and this is actually pretty fitting.

I'm upvoting tentatively because I think it's a good idea, but you need to work on the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the heroin idea, maybe when the top is spinning it gives the spinner a euphoric feeling, so they want to keep spinning it, keep the anti-ageing too, so when it spins the spinner is super high in paradise and they don't age at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Say… If you do end up going a little darker, you could have it as it causes the person/animal nearest the spinner to age at twice the normal rate, and if that being dies, whoever is currently spinning the top gets a little backflow and ages like six months in 5 seconds… Or you could make spinning it more addictive than Heroin, but instead of stopping the aging process, it reverses it until all you are is a baby grasping at a top, but the effects last awhile longer than the top spins, and the spinner is left as a fetus flopping and twitching slightly on the ground.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the advice. I tried to keep this one as minimal as possible, after my last one ended up a little verbose due to stuffing so much in. Looking at the comments here it clearly needs a rewrite to make the effects more understandable, and as you said, perhaps a little more unpleasantness. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has promise, but i think i see where the issue is. The containment side seems a little light, but as it's not all that dangerous, that makes sense. A life-protection top…not seen too many of those, and the concept is interesting…i think it needs a little more, however. I'm a grimdark kind of person, so the idea that injuries or time suspended during the spinning of the top suddenly happen all at once the second the top stops spinning…not saying that's the best plan, but i think it would spice it a bit more. Maybe a test log could help…in addition, moving the "history" paragraph to the Addendum might help as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He spun the top an awful lot. He was a lot older than he looked. QED. And he must have been spinning it all his life to have got back fifty odd years, but I imagine the close to death you get the more real the risk becomes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I get what you're going for, but why did he wait until he was so old to start spinning it? And why would anyone think to check if the top stopped people from aging?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't say medical testing shows him to be so old, it was his birth certificate. :-) And it stops you aging while it's spinning but you still have to take into account the non-spinning time, which was around 70 years. He's got to the end of his allotted years and he's delaying the inevitable by spinning the top. Imagine feeling the group of death upon you and knowing it's only the top spinning keeping it away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't, though—because if the top halts aging, then not only would he look young, he would actually be young, and medical testing would not show him to be one hundred. Furthermore, if he has been spinning this top long enough that there's a serious discrepancy between his physical and chronological age, then why does he die so quickly after dropping the thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Again I think a rewrite is needed to make this clearer. They found a man well over 100 who looked considerably younger who had horribly injured himself trying to get to a top he had been spinning obsessively for weeks- of all this SCPs faults I'd say that is pretty clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt that he only started using it obsessively once he realised he was dying, and had been so close to death that stopping long enough to be trying to get it from under the sofa was enough for him to die. I'm going to have to rewrite this one a lot as there is a lot I thought would be better left unsaid that isn't coming through in my text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The style of top shown in the picture is spun by wrapping the string around it and then pulling to spin the top as the string unwinds. The string is held tightly when pulling, that's where I anticipated the rope burn occurring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason the way it was phrased made me think it would cost much more then one D-class per test which was likely what confused me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: deValmont, even if I'm watching a top spin for 14 hours, /what makes me think that I'm not aging during those 14 hours/? Why would anyone even think to check that it had the additional effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless there's a method of top-spinning involving the palm of one hand that I don't know about, I can only conclude the callouses are from repeated use and the friction burn is from… trying to stop it? I have no idea why he would do that, though, hence my confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just seems like a very short amount of time to be able to tell that anything's happened at all. The enzymic breakthingy sounds good to me, though. (I obviously have no idea what you're talking about, but I can take some technobabble!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How else would you test what it prevented other than by getting a D-class to spin it and then doing horrible things to them to see if the top protected them? Oh, the fun you could have if not restricted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to put in detail about the noticed lack of enzymic breakdown in the cells of the user which would normally indicate ageing, but I felt it was too much detail. I'll put it in if people think it's valid. As for the 14 hours, I figure you start a top you wait for it to finish, and if it took hours you'd want to check how long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Believe me, I have no time for any of that Von Daniken bullshit. The AM was made well within the technological abilities of the time and its function is pedestrian enough. What I find unusual is how technically isolated it is. There's no simpler models or more advanced pieces that followed on from it. It just sits alone in the tech tree for that era, to use the Civ terminology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The friction burn would be from repeated use, and I like that idea—but I can't imagine how they would notice its effects, or who had the idea to spin a top for 14 hours in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thing is, if he uses it his whole life, he's not aging, right? So when it goes under the sofa, why does he dislocate his arm and die rather than take the time to move the couch? I would ask how he got old if he has this thing, but I think the area of effect is poorly calibrated. How would you even notice that you weren't aging if you were just playing with this thing? The problem with the human strength thing is that it's /unscientific/- "realistic limits" is a very subjective term and these are supposed to be lab documents. It's a tone issue and that's not the only place where you go off track.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do it over and over again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I agree, it feels tacked on and unnecessary. And I may have missed something, but I don't get the significance of the guy's friction burn in the recovery log.

I also feel like a 14 hour maximum isn't really enough time for them to actually be able to figure out what the thing does. How obvious can it be that someone isn't aging?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is the unavoidable impact to the D-class population the containment procedures talk about?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Antikythera Mechanism isn't "vaguely SCP-ish", unless you're one of the folks who cling tightly to the "people that long ago CAN'T HAVE DONE THIS because they were that long ago" style of thinking. This is the same path that, taken to an extreme, leads to the "Aliens built the pyramids because obviously people in ancient Egypt can't have had any level of dedication or precision in their work" mindset. We know what it is, we know how it works, we know what it's supposed to do, it was just the exact method of its construction that was lost to time. It's a case of "Holy crap they were better at this than we thought," not "OMG guyz ROMAN MECHANICAL MAGIC!"

That said, I don't have an issue with this thing being old, or relating to an actual semi-mysterious thing, but this has *nothing* to do with the AM, nor does that actually seem to have any impact on the object's current status; you may as well have a chunk of stone from the construction of the Leaning Tower of Pisa to which someone later glued a resin gargoyle novelty statue, and changed "As long as it's spun" into "As long as the block is balanced on one's head".

The object itself is… Decent, I guess is the best word I have for it, but I agree that the particulars of that really don't add at all to the item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something made of aged bronze that behaves weirdly being made out of bronze from a highly mysterious and vaguely SCP-ish real world item - I'm sorry, I wouldn't agree that it's embellishment, for me it's the crux of the SCP. It wouldn't do if we all thought the same though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are imperfections in the alloy and tooling marks on the inside of the top that suggest strongly that at some point the bronze was used as part of the Antikythera Mechanism.

This is both a non-sequitur and a needless embellishment. Ditch it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is indeed far less florid. Done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On being spun by a human being, it would appear that

could be

When SCP-1113 is spun by a human, it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, it's not compulsive in any anomalous way, just psychologically addictive. I wasn't sure about that, thank you for making the call.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I know that much… it seems it doesn't have much of a hook though, except maybe the desperate-ness people may come across when they're old…

I don't know why compulsion is in the tags when it doesn't compel. I'm removing it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Hmm, I hadn't really thought about the porcelain lady, I'm not sure how it would fit in without overcomplicating it. It did influence the name 'Lachesis' top', however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… I like it!
Although, I wonder. Does the fact that it bears a feminine figure at the top have anything to do with it's effects? If so, how? But then again, I'm just the sort of person who looks for things that tie in ability to appearance. Not always the most accurate with assumptions, I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the amount of explaining I'm having to do is suggestive that I haven't written it very well. You don't age when it's actually spinning, so if you're very near death, you would have to do nothing but spin it to ensure you survived, as if you stopped for even a short time you risk dying. It's not physically compulsive, but the alternative is to die.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not scary or anything to me. I might be tired and missing something though. It's a top that, when you spin it, you don't age?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Changed that sentence to 'using force within the realistic limits of human strength'.

I didn't visualise it as being compulsory to keep spinning it, I saw it more that you spin it, you don't die of old age. Hence the old guy who has probably used it on and off his whole life is now spinning it constantly because he knows that his time is short. He is not compelled in any supernatural way, he just doesn't want to die. Then it spins under the sofa.

The diary entries are a definite possibility, or I also considered some sort of folk tale passed down generations of his family, either way I wanted to get a short snappy version up and see what people thought would add to it, so thanks for the suggestions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Phrases like "within the limits of human strength" are not scientific. I'd play up either the compulsive aspect or have the old man's diary entries where he talks about becoming dependent on the thing. Make clear that once you spin it for a few hours you need to keep spinning it, forever, or Time will notice that you're cheating and return with a vengeance…
